problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A (continuous): model how bathwater in a single-faucet tub cools over time and devise a strategy for keeping it as close as possible to a preferred temperature by adjusting the inflow of hot water, accounting for tub shape/volume/surface area, material, additives (bubble bath), and the person in the tub; assess whether the strategy is even achievable. Team 54164's answer: a lumped (well-mixed) thermal ODE combining Newton's Law of Cooling with a faucet-heating term, an empirically-fit cooling-constant model, and a Python time-stepping simulation that selects the optimal flow rate by minimizing temperature deviance. Recommended optimal flow rate for a standard 60x30x14 in tub: 1.81 gallons/min (~11% of max).
models_used: Newton's Law of Cooling as a first-order linear ODE dT/dt = -kT (T measured relative to ambient), solved analytically by separation of variables to T = T0 e^(-kt) for the regression form
Faucet-heating term from the specific-heat/calorimetry balance dQ = m*Cp*dT, with simultaneous inflow of 48C water and overflow drainage at tub temperature, netting dT/dt = (dm/dt)(T_hot - T)/m_tub
Combined lumped-parameter energy-balance ODE: dT/dt = -k T + (dm/dt)(T_hot - T)/m_tub (well-mixed tub assumption)

Empirical dimensional model for the cooling constant k as a function of geometry: k = 0.3854 * e^(-0.1671 * cbrt(V^2 / SA_E^3)), fit by nonlinear regression (R^2 ~ 0.99930) across 4 datasets (three beakers + one self-run bathtub experiment)
Forward Euler / discrete time-stepping simulation (1-minute frames over 40 min) in Python to integrate the ODE
Brute-force grid search / 1-D optimization over flow rates (0.02 to 16 GPM in 0.01 steps) minimizing a deviance objective
Sum-of-squared-deviations objective (total deviance, written as Sum (x_n - xbar)^2) as the optimality criterion
Effective-surface-area insulation model: poorly-insulated faces counted as fractional exposed area (fiberglass at 1/8 of acrylic; bubble bath as a 0.95 surface-area multiplier)

Autodesk Inventor 3D CAD models of alternative tub geometries (cylindrical, triangular, square, hemisphere, oval, quarter-circle, trapezoidal) for the sensitivity study and for the 'Home Jacuzzi' product blueprint
key_techniques: Decomposed the system into exactly two physical effects (ambient cooling + faucet heating) under a 'five-constituent thermodynamic system' framing, then justified dropping heat exchange with walls/person/incoming-water via explicit assumptions

Ran their own real-world experiment during the competition (recorded a real bathtub cooling 37.0->33.2C over 60 min) to get a large-volume k-value, because published cooling data only existed for small beakers

Dimensional-analysis-driven feature engineering: argued k must be unitless, so it should depend on the unit-canceling group V^2/SA_E^3, then tested transformations (raw, squared, sqrt, cube-root) and picked cube-root by best R^2
Validated the simulation against their own experiment retroactively, showing the ODE simulation (R^2 = 0.99984) beat a plain exponential regression (R^2 = 0.97326)

Reframed the trigger logic physically: faucet stays off until water drops 1.4C (the human just-noticeable temperature difference from Davies et al.), tying a modeling threshold to a cited sensory fact
Sensitivity analysis sweeping tub geometry along two axes (vary surface area at constant volume; vary volume at constant surface area) plus material and additive variations

Translated heat-distribution (the spatial mixing sub-problem they could not solve analytically) into an actionable user behavior (counter-clockwise hand-swirling) plus an engineered product (Home Jacuzzi tube) instead of a PDE/CFD model
Tied results to concrete, interpretable numbers and units throughout (1.81 GPM = ~11% of max; deviance in degC^2; GPM faucet cap of 16)
why_it_won: "It is a model-rich, end-to-end, fully closed-loop solution that a non-expert judge can follow from physics to a single actionable number. It nails the MCM virtues: (1) a clean, physically-motivated governing equation derived from first principles with every step shown; (2) genuine original data collection (running their own bathtub cooling experiment mid-competition) to overcome a real data gap, which signals initiative and rigor; (3) explicit validation showing the model beats a naive baseline on R^2; (4) a creative-but-defensible empirical k-model via dimensional analysis; (5) thorough multi-axis sensitivity analysis covering every factor the prompt named (shape, volume, surface area, material, additives, person); (6) it answered ALL parts of the prompt, including the awkward 'distribute heat evenly' part, by pivoting to a behavioral strategy plus an invented product rather than ignoring it; and (7) outstanding, engaging communication with a strong non-technical explanation, a memorable voice/title, and full reproducibility (appendix experimental protocol + complete Python code). The blend of correct-enough physics, real experimentation, completeness, and readability is exactly the profile judges reward."
section_highlights: "Strong title/voice: 'The Math Behind the Bath: A Cool Investigation on Cooling' plus a plain-English '1.1 Non-Technical Explanation' that restates the whole solution for a layperson ('turn the faucet to about a ninth of its max'). The abstract is a true executive summary: states the two-part strategy, the headline number (1.81 GPM, 11% of max), and the qualitative findings (better insulation and additives lower the optimal flow; volume/surface area show no clean trend) up front. The model derivation is laid out step-by-step (separation of variables shown line by line; calorimetry balance built up from Q1 inflow and Q2 drainage). The k-regression section narrates the search transparently, reporting R^2 for each candidate transformation so the reader sees why cube-root was chosen. Validation section directly compares simulation vs regression R^2. Sensitivity analysis is visually organized: each tub variant gets a CAD render + a deviance-vs-flow-rate curve + a labeled result. Clean Strengths/Limitations/Future Work structure, a reproducible Appendix A experimental protocol, and full Appendix B Python source. The 'Home Jacuzzi' invention (with Inventor blueprint and renders) gives a memorable, tangible payoff."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The model is lumped/well-mixed (0-D), so it cannot actually model the spatial temperature non-uniformity that is half the problem; they assume the hand-swirling makes temperature 'perfectly even and instantaneous' and then sidestep the spatial PDE/CFD entirely (acknowledged in limitations)

The cooling-constant k-model is fit from only 4 data points (three beakers + one bathtub), with a huge extrapolation gap between beaker scale (0.1-0.8 L) and tub scale (~270 L); a 4-point fit hitting R^2 ~ 0.9993 is over-fit and the reported k is quoted to absurd precision (0.385387190197681, 'accuracy R^2=0.99929854436231100000')

Unit/derivation inconsistencies: the cooling ODE is written dT/dt = -kT but the simulation code uses (temperature - ROOM_TEMPERATURE), i.e. T is really the excess over ambient; equations 22 and 19 are mis-typeset (the heating term grouping is ambiguous in the text)

The objective is defined as deviance from the INITIAL temperature, not from the stated target body temperature (37C) explicitly, and the deviance-vs-flow plots have a non-physical kink (best near ~1.8-2 GPM then monotonically rising), suggesting the search granularity/curve shape wasn't deeply interrogated
Integration is crude forward-Euler at a 1-minute step with no step-size/stability check; the heating term is applied as a per-minute finite jump which can overshoot

Ambient temperature assumed constant (admitted); person assumed in perfect thermal equilibrium and treated as 'extra water'; the chosen fiberglass 1/8 insulation factor and bubble-bath 0.95 factor are stipulated, not derived or sourced

Several embedded data tables in the markdown appear OCR-garbled (e.g. the beaker regression table and some deviance values), and the swirl-direction 'counter-clockwise' claim is asserted without fluid-dynamics justification
The Python code is Python 2 (print statements), has indentation that would not run as transcribed, and would not be independently reproducible without cleanup
reusable_patterns: Two-term energy-balance template for any 'keep something at a setpoint' continuous problem: dState/dt = (natural decay toward environment) + (control input effect), here Newton cooling + faucet heating; reusable for HVAC, batteries, chemical reactors, etc.

When published data only covers the wrong scale, run your own quick experiment during the competition and document a reproducible protocol in an appendix; it converts a data gap into an initiative-and-rigor signal

Dimensional-analysis feature engineering for an empirical constant: argue the target must be dimensionless, construct the unit-canceling dimensionless group (V^2/SA^3 here), then test a small family of transforms and report R^2 to justify the pick
Always validate the mechanistic model against a held-out/real dataset AND against a naive baseline (regression), reporting both R^2 values to prove the model adds value
Tie a modeling threshold to a cited human/physical fact (the 1.4C just-noticeable-difference triggering the faucet) so model choices read as principled, not arbitrary

Define a single scalar optimality objective (sum of squared deviations from setpoint) and select the control by a simple grid search, then plot the objective-vs-control curve so the optimum is visually obvious
Structure sensitivity analysis as controlled one-factor-at-a-time sweeps (vary A at constant B, then B at constant A) with a small results table/curve per case

When a sub-problem resists clean math (spatial mixing), pivot to an actionable recommendation (user behavior) plus an engineered artifact (the Home Jacuzzi product with CAD blueprint) — answer every part of the prompt even if not every part is a equation
Lead with a layperson 'Non-Technical Explanation' and an abstract that states the headline number in interpretable units (e.g. '11% of max flow'); use a memorable title/voice to aid judge readability
Maximize reproducibility: include the experimental protocol and the full simulation source code in appendices
